content:
To find the area of trapezoid $ABCD,$ we'll first need to find the side lengths of $ABCD$ using the information given about its perimeter and the incenters of triangles $ABC$ and $DBC$. We are given that the perimeter of trapezoid $ABCD$ is 120, so the sum of all 4 of its side lengths is equal to 120.

We're given that $\triangle ABC$ has perimeter 114 and $AD  BC.$ We are also given that the distance between the incenter of $\triangle ABC$ and the incenter of $\triangle DBC$ is $16.$  Let the incenter of $\triangle ABC$ be I. As the incenter, I is the intersection of the angle bisectors of $\triangle ABC$. Hence, AI is the angle bisector of $\angle BAC$. 

We're first going to find the value of $AD$. The trapezoid's height is equal to the difference between $AI$ (the height of $\triangle ABD$) and $ID$ (the height of $\triangle BDC$), where $I$ is the incenter of the trapezoid and also the incenter of both triangles $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DBC$.

Let $x = AD$. We can then express the perimeter of the trapezoid as
\[120=x+6+8+BC\]
\[\because BC \approx x+6\] 

We can use angle bisector theorem to calculate $AB$:
\[AB\cdot AC = 7^2\]
\[AB\cdot (AC-x)=7^2\]
\[AB\frac{(AC \text{ and } x)\cdot BC}{AC}\]
\[AB \approx \sqrt{(BC^2)}\]

If $BC \approx x+ 6$ we can solve the equation
\[AB \approx \sqrt{(x+6)^2}\]

As we calculate the $AB, AD $ to get $AD \approx \frac{20}{3} $ and $ AB \approx \sqrt{(7)^2 + \frac{4}{9}(8)^2}$

We know the height of $\triangle ABD$ is the difference between $AI$ (the height of $\triangle ABD$) and $ID$ (the height of $\triangle BCD$) is equal to

As we know, the angle bisectors of the base angles  (in isoseles $\triangle ABC$, $\angle ADC$, and $\angle ABC$) of triangle $\triangle ABC$ also bisect its height $AH$. This means that the ratio of these line segments, namely $\frac{AH}{AI}=\frac{BH}{BI},$ will be equal to the ratio of their corresponding base angles $\frac{AH}{AH-12}\propto\frac{AH}{AI}$. We know this because the angle bisectors of a triangle are the lines that divide two sides of any triangle in the ratio of the other two sides of the triangle, hence the line segment $AI$ divides the base $BC$ into the ratio of sides of the triangle $\triangle ABI$. Now if $AI=AI_0$, then $\frac{AH}{AH-12}=\frac{\frac{AI_0\cdot AH}{AI_0}}{AI_0-AI_0}=\frac{AH}{AH-AI_0}=\frac{AI_0}{AI_0-12}$. Thus $AI_0$ can be calculated, as we already know the side lengths, using ratio method. We know that $$\frac{AH}{AI_0}=\frac{AH-12}{AI_0-12}$$. This implies

\[AI_0\propto \frac{(AH)(AI_0-12)}{(AI_0)}(AH-12)=14 \frac{(AH)(AI_0-12)}{(AI_0)}= 14  $ which is equal to $ AI_0\cdot\frac{AI_0-12}{AH}\propto 14$$

Now that we know the ratio of $AI_0$ and know that $AI_0  16$. We can also calculate $AH$ using the same ratio method

\[\frac{AH}{AH-12}=14\cdot\frac{AI_0}{AI_0-12}\cdot\frac{AH-12}{AH}\propto \frac{AI}{AI-12}\]

We have 
$$AI_0 \leq 16 $$. We can use trial and error, with the value of $AI_0  16$, and we have 
$\boxed{AI_0=14}$

Thus, height = $AH-12 = 14  AI_0$.

\begin{aligned}
 &\textbf{}\\
&AI_{0}\propto \frac{AH}{AI_0}=14\\
&AI_0 \cdot \frac{AI_0-12}{AH} \propto 14\\
&\textbf{}\\
\end{aligned}
